title: The Hormuz shock: what the data reveals about global oil markets in 2026 | Wood Mackenzie
gist: The effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz in early May 2026 shut in ~11 million b/d at peak, the biggest oil supply disruption in a generation; Wood Mackenzie argues the real trading edge came from gaps between public announcements and what physical data (satellite, storage, pipeline, vessel) actually showed.

insights.claim: The effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz in early May 2026 triggered the most significant oil supply disruption in a generation, with Wood Mackenzie's High Frequency Oil Production Monitor tracking approximately 11 million barrels per day of production shut in at peak.
insights.speaker: Alan Gelder
insights.so_what: An 11 Mb/d peak shut-in defines the magnitude of the 2026 supply shock and the baseline for recovery trades.
insights.quote: tracked approximately 11 million barrels per day of production shut in at peak
insights.locator: The effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz

insights.claim: The gap between what public announcements implied and what physical data actually showed was where the most consequential trading decisions were made during the Hormuz shock.
insights.speaker: Alan Gelder
insights.so_what: Edge in crisis oil trading comes from proprietary physical intelligence (satellite/storage/vessel), not headlines.
insights.quote: the gap between what public announcements implied and what physical data actually showed was where the most consequential trading decisions were made

insights.claim: When Iraq and Kuwait announced shut-ins the market reacted immediately, but Wood Mackenzie's satellite flaring and storage data revealed a physical picture quite different from what price action implied, raising whether traders got ahead of the actual barrels lost.
insights.speaker: Alan Gelder
insights.so_what: Suggests the market may have overpriced the Gulf cut versus real physical loss; a fade signal for those with the data.
insights.locator: Did the market price the Gulf supply cut too fast?

themes.theme: Crude supply restructuring: Canada as Asia's swing barrel

insights.claim: Asian refiners moved quickly to replace lost Persian Gulf barrels with Canadian grades and TMX flows responded accordingly, but oil sands production growth is on a collision course with takeaway capacity by 2027, putting the durability of Canada's role as Asia's swing crude supplier in question.
insights.speaker: Alan Gelder
insights.so_what: Whether pipeline expansions relieve the 2027 takeaway constraint determines if Canada's Asian market share is structural or temporary.
insights.locator: Is Canada's emergence as Asia's swing crude supplier

insights.claim: California faces a refined-product crunch whose resolution depends on which infrastructure solutions are deployed, flagged as a distinct downstream stress point in the post-Hormuz market.
insights.speaker: Alan Gelder
insights.so_what: West Coast product tightness is a localized dislocation to watch for refining-margin and infrastructure plays.
insights.locator: What is driving California's refined product crunch

insights.claim: European jet and diesel stocks may not recover before winter demand peaks, a middle-distillate risk heading into the 2026-27 heating season.
insights.speaker: Alan Gelder
insights.so_what: A distillate stock shortfall into winter is bullish for European refining margins and diesel cracks.
insights.locator: Will European jet and diesel stocks recover

insights.claim: Asia-Pacific is restructuring its crude supply, with demand destruction expected to become visible somewhere first as high prices bite.
insights.speaker: Alan Gelder
insights.so_what: Tracking where APAC demand destruction first appears gives an early read on how far the price shock has propagated.
insights.locator: How is Asia-Pacific restructuring its crude supply
